summary: Gruber pushes back against the widespread criticism that AirPods are 'destined to be lost,' arguing that people naturally take better care of expensive items. He shares two personal close calls from months of daily use, both resolved quickly because he was attentive. He recommends always carrying the case and treating AirPods as valuable objects. A reader's anecdote about fancy fountain pens perfectly reinforces his point: once you invest in something nice, you stop losing it.
key_points: The 'easily lost' criticism of AirPods ignores human nature — people are more careful with things they value
If AirPods were cheap and bundled with iPhones, they'd be lost far more often than as a $159 standalone product
The practical solution is simple discipline: buds go in the case, in your ears, or in your fingers — never set down loose
The fancy fountain pen analogy perfectly captures the psychology: investing in something nice changes how carefully you treat it
money_quotes: It's human nature to be more careful with valuable items.
If AirPods cost $29 and were included in the box with iPhones, they'd be lost more frequently than they are as a $159 standalone product.
The buds are either in the case, in my ears, or in my fingers.
In short, the best way not to lose them is to treat them as easily lost, valuable objects.
"I think you'll find that once you have a nice one, you won't lose it."
The danger wasn't really that I'd lose it, but that someone else would step on it before I got to it.
key_insight: The price of AirPods is itself a defense against losing them — people naturally take better care of things they've invested in.
